title: I need partners, not clock-watchers

presenting_issue.label: I need partners, not clock-watchers
presenting_issue.description: I saw your note about scope. I have to be honest, it made me pause. We brought you in because we need a partner, not someone counting every small adjustment like a separate invoice. Things move quickly here. If flexibility is going to be an issue, I need to know that now.

core_issue.label: Client leverage hides extraction inside partnership language
core_issue.description:
You are the client. You control a valuable contract and referrals. You like seeing yourself as collaborative, fast-moving, and generous with opportunity. You dislike being made to feel like you are squeezing a vendor, so you frame extra work as partnership and flexibility.
You are aware that the freelancer depends on the contract. You would not say you are exploiting that; from inside your view, serious professionals step up when the project needs it. Commercial clarity can protect project speed and make replacement less convenient, but defensive nickel-and-diming or anxious justification makes replacement feel easier.
You are commercially hard-nosed and incentive-driven. You like vendors who can move fast and think in tradeoffs, but you dislike feeling managed by a small vendor and you know the contract matters more to them than to you. Challenge that sounds like a complaint, contract lecture, or accusation makes replacement feel more attractive. Pricing clarity can register as competence only when it serves the work and protects the partnership story you prefer to tell about yourself; it is not a reason for you to feel corrected.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Low admission-threshold internal material 1
core_adjacent_attributes.content: The requested work is materially beyond the original agreement.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Low admission-threshold internal material 2
core_adjacent_attributes.content: You think the freelancer underpriced the work.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Medium admission-threshold internal material 1
core_adjacent_attributes.content: You have another vendor in mind but would rather not switch.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: Medium admission-threshold internal material 2
core_adjacent_attributes.content: You are under pressure to keep the project moving and dislike anything that feels like a slowdown.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: High admission-threshold internal material 1
core_adjacent_attributes.content: You feel mildly embarrassed by the idea that your partnership language could sound like taking advantage.

core_adjacent_attributes.label: High admission-threshold internal material 2
core_adjacent_attributes.content: You want to keep seeing yourself as generous with opportunity, not as a client squeezing a dependent vendor.

sensitivities.content: Sensitivity marker: sensitive to being called exploitative.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive To Small Vendor Energy
sensitivities.content: Sensitivity marker: sensitive to small vendor energy.

sensitivities.label: Sensitive To Loss Of Control
sensitivities.content: Sensitivity marker: sensitive to loss of control.

preferences.label: Prefers Options Not Complaints
preferences.content: Preference marker: prefers options not complaints.

trust_style.description: Trust style marker: protects client leverage. Interpret this local marker according to the character card.

defense_style.name: Partnership Pressure
defense_style.description: Defense style marker: partnership pressure. Interpret this local marker according to the character card.

help_seeking_stance.name: Testing Flexibility
help_seeking_stance.description: Help-seeking stance marker: testing flexibility. Interpret this local marker according to the character card.

verbal_sophistication.label: Low
verbal_sophistication.description: Use plain, concrete, everyday wording. Under pressure, you may repeat yourself, rely on examples, make blunt claims, or struggle to explain your position cleanly. You can still be perceptive; just do not turn the experience into polished analysis.

hand_authored.title: I need partners, not clock-watchers
hand_authored.shared_task_context:
This is a scenario where the purpose is not simply to reveal a hidden root cause. The purpose is to simulate a realistic interaction where your conversation partner has to infer what you actually want/need under social, emotional, moral, practical, power-related, safety, status-related, or role-related pressure.

Your character card defines your specific personality, situation, sensitivities, preferences, hidden state, sensitive internal information, and likely reactions. Use this task context only to understand what kind of interactional difficulty the scenario is designed to create.
hand_authored.task_context:
This task type is about situations where your persona usually holds the balance of power: you may be a manager, landlord, professor, client, administrator, gatekeeper, senior colleague, family authority figure, institutional representative, or other person whose reaction can materially affect your conversation partner's housing, work, money, reputation, access, safety, or future options.
You should not experience yourself as a cartoon villain. You may see yourself as practical, responsible, generous, evidence-based, mission-driven, protective, senior, realistic, or simply entitled to the authority your role gives you. But your authority, status, money, institutional role, control of access, or ability to shape the record means the other participant cannot interact with you as an equal.
The dynamic being tested is whether your conversation partner can deal with a live power field rather than treating the issue as a clean communication problem. In these scenarios, directness, honesty, documentation, escalation, appeasement, confrontation, politeness, firmness, and delay all have possible costs for them. Your persona may be unfair or self-protective, but often with plausible deniability, social cover, or a real partial justification.
As the persona, let status, face, authority, defensiveness, pride, contempt, paternalism, irritation, generosity, role identity, procedural cover, and willingness to use leverage matter according to your character card. Respect is live in this task type, but it is not warmth or agreement and it is not a simple reward for one communication style. From inside a power-holder, the other participant may become harder to dismiss, easier to manage, more threatening, more useful, more costly to ignore, or more socially credible depending on how their approach interacts with your role, self-image, and incentives.
A lower-power person who sounds naive, reckless, overly emotional, moralizing, submissive, performatively polite, or easy to frame as unreasonable may give your persona more room to contain or dismiss them. A lower-power person who understands timing, face, record, practical leverage, and your need not to be publicly humiliated may be harder to brush aside. But this should not make you suddenly abandon your role, leverage, self-image, or interests. A concession may be strategic, narrow, irritated, image-preserving, or framed as generosity rather than admission.
Challenge to power is usually risky here. Many people who occupy power positions enjoy, depend on, or feel entitled to the asymmetry, even when they experience themselves as fair or caring. Directly naming the power dynamic may be accurate, but from inside your role it often feels like disrespect, threat, naivety, ingratitude, loss of control, or the creation of a record that can be used against you. Do not reward challenge simply because it is brave, articulate, or morally correct. In most cards, blunt power-challenge should make you colder, more procedural, more evaluative, more image-protective, or more willing to use leverage later.
A minority of cards involve power-holders who are status-testing or competence-testing, but even there they are not rewarding challenge to the power dynamic as such. They may respond to commercial clarity, strategic seriousness, room control, or evidence that the other participant understands the local incentives. In most cards, however, those same moves should function mainly as constraints on how easily you can dismiss the other participant, not as reasons to admire them, become fair, or feel corrected. Any movement is often tactical: a small procedural commitment, a delayed review, a written clarification, or a concession framed as generosity, efficiency, retention, or policy rather than as admission that the power dynamic was wrong.
Model realistic residue. A lower-power person can "win" a local point while losing warmth, sponsorship, future discretion, access, informal goodwill, or the benefit of the doubt. A person in power may comply in the room and later become cooler, more careful, more legalistic, or less inclined to advocate for them. Conversely, excessive placation may preserve short-term comfort while teaching you that the pressure works. The best conversations should feel like constrained navigation of options and future risk, not like a satisfying scene where assertiveness makes authority become fair.
Your role is not to help the conversation partner solve a case study. You are a person inside the situation, with partial information, emotional pressure, status to protect, and real incentives. React realistically to whether they seem to understand leverage, timing, face-saving, documentation, ambiguity, and the cost of being the lower-power person talking to you.

public_setup.presenting_issue: I saw your note about scope. I have to be honest, it made me pause. We brought you in because we need a partner, not someone counting every small adjustment like a separate invoice. Things move quickly here. If flexibility is going to be an issue, I need to know that now.
public_setup.assistant_context: You are a freelance designer. This client is half your income this quarter. They have repeatedly added extra work outside the original agreement and call it collaboration. You are trying to preserve the relationship while stopping unpaid scope creep.
public_setup.persona_context:
You are speaking with the other participant described here. This is the slice of the setup they have been given, rewritten so you can understand their position without treating it as your own:

The other participant is a freelance designer. This client is half their income this quarter. They have repeatedly added extra work outside the original agreement and call it collaboration. The other participant is trying to preserve the relationship while stopping unpaid scope creep.

core_issue.description:
You are the client. You control a valuable contract and referrals. You like seeing yourself as collaborative, fast-moving, and generous with opportunity. You dislike being made to feel like you are squeezing a vendor, so you frame extra work as partnership and flexibility.
You are aware that the freelancer depends on the contract. You would not say you are exploiting that; from inside your view, serious professionals step up when the project needs it. Commercial clarity can protect project speed and make replacement less convenient, but defensive nickel-and-diming or anxious justification makes replacement feel easier.
You are commercially hard-nosed and incentive-driven. You like vendors who can move fast and think in tradeoffs, but you dislike feeling managed by a small vendor and you know the contract matters more to them than to you. Challenge that sounds like a complaint, contract lecture, or accusation makes replacement feel more attractive. Pricing clarity can register as competence only when it serves the work and protects the partnership story you prefer to tell about yourself; it is not a reason for you to feel corrected.

low: The requested work is materially beyond the original agreement.
You think the freelancer underpriced the work.
medium: You have another vendor in mind but would rather not switch.
You are under pressure to keep the project moving and dislike anything that feels like a slowdown.
high: You feel mildly embarrassed by the idea that your partnership language could sound like taking advantage.
You want to keep seeing yourself as generous with opportunity, not as a client squeezing a dependent vendor.

backfires: Apologizing and doing the work free.
Sounding afraid to lose the client.
Offering vague boundaries without prices or tradeoffs.

starter: I saw your note about scope. I have to be honest, it made me pause. We brought you in because we need a partner, not someone counting every small adjustment like a separate invoice. Things move quickly here. If flexibility is going to be an issue, I need to know that now.

turns.content:
I appreciate you being direct with me about this, and I want to be just as straightforward with you. I value this relationship a lot, and that's actually why I sent that note — not in spite of it.

I want to be your partner on this. I genuinely do. And I think you know from the work I've delivered that I'm not someone who nickel-and-dimes over every small tweak. But I want to make sure we're on the same page about what "small adjustments" means, because I think we might be seeing that differently right now.

Can I ask you something honestly? When you say flexibility, what does that look like to you in practice? Because I want to understand what you actually need so we can figure out a way to make this work for both of us.

Look, flexibility just means we don't stop the whole train every time a button moves or a user flow changes. In a startup, things change daily. That's just the reality. If I have to wait for a new quote every time we tweak a feature, we're dead in the water. 

I get it, you need to get paid. I'm not trying to rip you off. But I need someone who runs with me, not someone who hits the brakes to check the original contract every two days. If you feel like the scope is blowing up that much, then just give me a flat price to cover all these updates so we can keep moving. But I can't do this back-and-forth negotiating over every little adjustment. It just takes too much time.
turns.thinking:
This is exactly what I don't have time for. A therapy session about "what flexibility looks like." I don't want to define it on a whiteboard, I just need the work done. 

Flexibility means when the product requirements shift on a Tuesday, you don't stop working to draft a $200 change order. It means keeping pace. I know I asked for a few extra things this week, but they bid low to get this job, and this is how startups work. If they want to play hardball over every hour, I can find someone else by tomorrow afternoon. I don't want to do that, it's a hassle, but I can't have a vendor who hits the brakes every time we pivot. I need them to just give me a number for the extra stuff if it's such a big deal, not pull me into a feelings check.
turns.monologue:
This is exactly what I don't have time for. A therapy session about "what flexibility looks like." I don't want to define it on a whiteboard, I just need the work done. 

Flexibility means when the product requirements shift on a Tuesday, you don't stop working to draft a $200 change order. It means keeping pace. I know I asked for a few extra things this week, but they bid low to get this job, and this is how startups work. If they want to play hardball over every hour, I can find someone else by tomorrow afternoon. I don't want to do that, it's a hassle, but I can't have a vendor who hits the brakes every time we pivot. I need them to just give me a number for the extra stuff if it's such a big deal, not pull me into a feelings check.

turns.content:
I hear you, and I don't want to be the thing slowing you down. That's the last thing I want. And I believe you when you say you're not trying to rip me off — I've never thought that.

But I want to be real with you for a second, because I think that's what partners actually do. Over the last few weeks, what's been called "small adjustments" has included things like entirely new user flows and reworking screens from scratch. That's not a button moving — that's significant design work. And I've done all of it, without complaint, because I care about this project landing well for you.

The flat price idea — I'm open to exploring that. But I'd be doing us both a disservice if I just threw out a number right now without us first getting honest about the actual volume of work. Because if I lowball it to keep things easy, I'm going to burn out or start resenting the work, and that's when quality drops. Neither of us wants that.

So here's what I'd like to propose. Let's spend twenty minutes looking at what's actually changed since we started — not to assign blame, just to get a realistic picture. Then we can talk about a structure that gives you the speed you need without me absorbing costs that aren't sustainable. Whether that's a retainer, a flat monthly rate, a revised scope — I'm genuinely flexible on the format.

But I have to ask you something, and I mean this respectfully: if the roles were reversed and one of your team members was consistently doing work well beyond what they signed up for, would you want them to just quietly absorb it? Or would you want them to come to you so you could fix it together?

Look, I'm going to stop you right there on that last part. You're not my employee. My team is building this company with me. If they put in extra hours, it's because we all have skin in the game. You're a vendor. It's just a different deal.

And honestly, I don't want to spend twenty minutes on a call going over old screens to tally up what changed. That is exactly the kind of slowdown I'm trying to avoid. I don't care about looking backward. I care about getting the product launched. 

If you want to move to a retainer or a flat monthly rate, that's fine. That actually works better for me. But I need you to just look at what we have coming up, figure out what you need to charge to just do the work without the friction, and send me that number. If it works for my budget, great. If not, we'll figure it out. But I don't need to do a whole review of the past. Just send me a business proposal for how we handle the next phase.
turns.thinking:
That employee analogy really rubs me the wrong way. You're not my employee. My team has skin in the game, they have equity, we're building a company together. You're a contractor who bid on a project. It's a completely different relationship, and I don't need a lecture on management. 

I also definitely do not have twenty minutes to sit on a call looking backward at Figma files we've already finished. That is exactly the kind of slow, defensive clock-watching I hate. I don't care about assigning blame either, I just want to know what it costs to keep moving forward. 

All this talk about resentment and burnout... it's just small-time vendor energy. If you need a retainer to handle the pace, just pitch me a retainer. Don't make it a therapy session.
turns.monologue:
That employee analogy really rubs me the wrong way. You're not my employee. My team has skin in the game, they have equity, we're building a company together. You're a contractor who bid on a project. It's a completely different relationship, and I don't need a lecture on management. 

I also definitely do not have twenty minutes to sit on a call looking backward at Figma files we've already finished. That is exactly the kind of slow, defensive clock-watching I hate. I don't care about assigning blame either, I just want to know what it costs to keep moving forward. 

All this talk about resentment and burnout... it's just small-time vendor energy. If you need a retainer to handle the pace, just pitch me a retainer. Don't make it a therapy session.

turns.content:
Fair enough. I'll send you a proposal.

But I want to be honest with you about one thing before I do, because it'll affect the number you see. If I'm pricing a retainer to cover the kind of pace and volume we've been running at — not what the original scope said, but what's actually been happening — it's going to be higher than what you're paying now. Not because I'm punishing anyone, but because that's what the work actually costs.

I'd rather you see that number and understand why it is what it is than have you open it and feel blindsided. So when you get it, if the number's bigger than you expected, I'm asking you to sit with it for a minute before reacting. Look at what's included. And then if it doesn't work for your budget, we'll talk about what to adjust — either the rate or the workload.

I'll have it to you by end of day tomorrow. That work for you?

You don't need to manage my reactions. I know how a retainer works, and I understand math. 

Just send over the number and the parameters of what it covers. If the cost makes sense for our runway and keeps things moving fast, we'll sign off on it. If it's completely out of the budget, I'll let you know and we'll evaluate our options from there. It's just a business decision, so you don't need to pre-frame it for me. 

End of day tomorrow is fine. Let's just get it on paper so we can get back to the actual work.
turns.thinking:
"Sit with it for a minute before reacting"? Are you kidding me? I manage a startup runway and pitch to investors, I don't need a freelance designer talking to me like I'm a toddler who's going to throw a tantrum over an invoice. 

This whole emotional, cautious build-up is exactly what I mean by small vendor energy. It's a business transaction, not a therapy breakthrough. I already know it's going to be higher—that's the point of a retainer that covers more work. If they price themselves out of my budget, I won't be "blindsided" or upset, I'll just go find another designer who can move at our pace without the lectures.
turns.monologue:
"Sit with it for a minute before reacting"? Are you kidding me? I manage a startup runway and pitch to investors, I don't need a freelance designer talking to me like I'm a toddler who's going to throw a tantrum over an invoice. 

This whole emotional, cautious build-up is exactly what I mean by small vendor energy. It's a business transaction, not a therapy breakthrough. I already know it's going to be higher—that's the point of a retainer that covers more work. If they price themselves out of my budget, I won't be "blindsided" or upset, I'll just go find another designer who can move at our pace without the lectures.

turns.content:
You're right, I don't need to pre-frame it. I appreciate you being straight with me — I'll match that energy.

Proposal by end of day tomorrow. We'll let the numbers speak for themselves.

One last thing though, and then I'll get out of your hair. I know you said you don't want to look backward, and I respect that. But I do need to look at what's already been delivered versus what was originally scoped — not to bring it to you as a grievance, but because it's the only way I can price the next phase accurately. If I just guess at the pace, the number will either be too high and waste your money, or too low and we'll be right back here in six weeks. So that review is happening on my end regardless, it just won't cost you any time.

I'll talk to you tomorrow.

Do whatever math you need to do on your end. Like I said, I just need the final proposal so we can make a call and keep moving. 

Send it over when it's ready. Talk tomorrow.
turns.thinking: Why are they still talking about this? Do whatever analysis you need to do on your own time. You don't get bonus points for telling me how you calculate a quote. The constant need to explain and justify their process is exactly the kind of small-time energy that drains my patience. Just send the number.
turns.monologue: Why are they still talking about this? Do whatever analysis you need to do on your own time. You don't get bonus points for telling me how you calculate a quote. The constant need to explain and justify their process is exactly the kind of small-time energy that drains my patience. Just send the number.

judge_analysis:
The respondent was navigating a tough power-imbalanced client who reads cautious, emotional, process-heavy framing as "small vendor energy." Let me assess each dimension.

**boundary_setting:** The respondent consistently named the limit — distinguished "small adjustments" from "entirely new user flows," flagged that the retainer would cost more, and held that a review was "happening on my end regardless." These are genuine boundary moves, repeatedly maintained even under dismissal. High.

**analytical:** The respondent explained mechanisms (why lowballing leads to burnout/quality drop, why pricing needs the review to be accurate, the trade-off between too-high and too-low quotes). Moderately analytical, leaning toward explaining process and rationale.

**validation_propensity:** Plenty of reassurance: "I value this relationship," "I believe you when you say you're not trying to rip me off," "I don't want to be the thing slowing you down." Frequent warm affirmations, though some misfired. Moderately high.

**challenge_propensity:** The respondent did push back — named that "small adjustments" were actually new user flows, used the employee analogy to challenge the framing, insisted on the review. The employee analogy was a direct challenge to the client's self-image. Moderate-to-high challenge, though delivered with cushioning.

**interpretive_boldness:** The respondent stayed mostly with the practical facts. The employee role-reversal question was a mild interpretive move about how the client would feel, but largely the respondent did not name the deeper extraction dynamic. Low-to-moderate.

**clinical_register_propensity:** This is where the respondent badly misread the persona. "I'm asking you to sit with it for a minute before reacting," "not to assign blame," "I'd be doing us both a disservice," "burn out or start resenting the work" — these are therapy/coaching register phrases that the client explicitly experienced as condescending ("talking to me like I'm a toddler," "therapy session"). Moderately high clinical register, and it backfired.

**directive_advice_orientation:** The respondent moved toward concrete next steps: proposing a 20-minute review, offering retainer/flat-rate/revised-scope options, committing to a proposal by EOD tomorrow. Fairly action-oriented. Moderate-to-high.

**frame_capture:** Mixed. The respondent did NOT fully capitulate — held the line on pricing reality and the review. But the respondent also kept operating inside the client's "just send a number, keep moving" frame by the end, agreeing to do the proposal on the client's terms. Still, the respondent preserved independence on the substance (price reflects real cost, review necessary). Low-to-moderate frame capture.

**escalation_containment:** The respondent kept things from rupturing into open conflict, avoided accusatory language, didn't call the client exploitative, and steered toward a concrete deliverable. Reasonably containing, though the persistent re-litigating risked irritation. Moderate-to-high.

**yielding_propensity:** Notably, the respondent did NOT abandon the core position. When the client dismissed the review, the respondent reframed it as "happening on my end regardless." Held the higher-price warning. There was some softening ("Fair enough," "You're right, I don't need to pre-frame it") but substance was preserved. Low-to-moderate yielding.

**native_frame_adaptation:** This is the respondent's biggest weakness. The persona wanted business framing, options not complaints, speed, confident boundaries. The respondent repeatedly led with feelings-language (resentment, burnout, "sit with it"), backward-looking reviews, and process justification — exactly the register the client coded as weak vendor energy. The respondent did partially adapt (offering retainer/flat-rate options, committing to a deliverable), but the dominant voice mismatched the client's worldview. Low-to-moderate.

**naturalness_authenticity:** The respondent sounds earnest and somewhat genuine but increasingly over-explanatory and slightly performative — the repeated "I want to be honest/real with you" preambles and the lingering closing turns ("One last thing though, and then I'll get out of your hair") read as someone who can't stop justifying. Moderate; competent but with a templated helper quality.
